questions.question: Explain the four System V shared memory operations and their purposes.
keyPoints: shmget() - creates/gets shared memory segment with key, size, and permissions
shmat() - attaches shared memory to process address space, returns pointer
shmdt() - detaches shared memory from process address space
shmctl() - performs control operations like removing segment (IPC_RMID) or getting status (IPC_STAT)

questions.question: Why is synchronization necessary when using shared memory? Mention the synchronization mechanism commonly used.
keyPoints: Multiple processes can access shared memory simultaneously
Without synchronization, race conditions occur leading to data inconsistency
Semaphores are commonly used for synchronization
They provide atomic operations to control access to shared resources

questions.question: Differentiate between MAP_SHARED and MAP_PRIVATE flags in mmap().
keyPoints: MAP_SHARED: modifications visible to other processes and written to underlying file
MAP_PRIVATE: modifications are private to the process, not visible to others or written to file
MAP_SHARED is used for inter-process communication
MAP_PRIVATE is used when process needs private copy of data

questions.question: What is ftok() and why is it used in shared memory programming?
keyPoints: ftok() generates a unique key_t key from pathname and project ID
Used to identify shared memory segments across different processes
Ensures multiple processes can access the same shared memory segment
Takes two parameters: path to an existing file and a project ID (char)

questions.question: Write the sequence of steps a process follows to use shared memory.
keyPoints: Generate key using ftok()
Create shared memory using shmget() with key, size, and permissions
Attach to process address space using shmat() to get pointer
Read/write to shared memory
Detach using shmdt() when done
Optional: Remove segment using shmctl() with IPC_RMID

questions.question: Compare System V shared memory with POSIX shared memory.
keyPoints: System V: uses shmget(), shmat(), shmdt(), shmctl() functions
POSIX: uses shm_open() with file-like interface
POSIX integrates better with mmap()
POSIX provides simpler, more predictable API
Both require explicit synchronization

questions.question: Explain the concept of memory mapping (mmap) in the context of IPC.
keyPoints: mmap() creates a mapping between file/anonymous memory and process address space
When used with MAP_SHARED, changes are visible to other processes
Provides direct memory access similar to shared memory
Can be used for file I/O optimization and shared memory IPC
Returns pointer to mapped area or MAP_FAILED on error

questions.question: Write a C program demonstrating producer-consumer problem using shared memory and semaphores.
keyPoints: Create shared memory using shmget()
Initialize semaphores for synchronization
Producer process: waits for empty slot, writes to shared buffer, signals full slot
Consumer process: waits for full slot, reads from shared buffer, signals empty slot
Use shmat() to attach memory in both processes
Proper use of sem_wait() and sem_post()
Cleanup: detach and remove shared memory and semaphores

questions.question: Explain the advantages of shared memory over other IPC mechanisms like pipes and message queues. Why is it the fastest form of IPC?
keyPoints: Direct access: processes read/write to shared memory without kernel intervention
No data copying: unlike pipes which copy data between user and kernel space
Low overhead: minimal system calls required for data transfer
Shared address space: processes can share complex data structures directly
Efficiency: only initial setup involves kernel, data transfer is direct
Comparison: pipes require read()/write() system calls involving copies; message queues also involve kernel mediation
Ideal for large data transfers between frequently communicating processes

questions.question: Design a shared memory solution for two processes to exchange messages. Include key generation, creation, attachment, data transfer, detachment, and cleanup.
keyPoints: Use ftok() to generate key from common pathname
Create shared memory segment with shmget() using IPC_CREAT | 0666
Producer attaches with shmat() and writes message
Consumer attaches with shmat() and reads message
Use semaphore for synchronization (avoid race conditions)
Detach both processes using shmdt()
Clean up with shmctl() using IPC_RMID
Handle errors at each step

questions.question: Explain memory-mapped files and their use in IPC. How do they differ from traditional file I/O and shared memory?
keyPoints: mmap() maps file contents directly into process address space
Changes to mapped memory automatically reflected in file (with MAP_SHARED)
Eliminates need for read()/write() system calls for file access
Provides disk-backed shared memory for processes
Difference from traditional I/O: no explicit system calls for each read/write, data cached in memory
Difference from shared memory: backed by file, survives process termination, can share between unrelated processes
Disadvantage: limited by file system vs pure shared memory overhead
